name: Retention type
description: Classifies the method used to measure user retention by defining the return logic applied to a cohort. It categorises retained users into standard analytical retention frameworks such as classic/unbounded retention, rolling retention, bracketed retention, and strict day-N retention so teams can compare retention results consistently across reports and tools.
formula: CASE WHEN retention_logic IN ('classic','unbounded') THEN 'Classic Retention' WHEN retention_logic IN ('rolling') THEN 'Rolling Retention' WHEN retention_logic IN ('bracketed','range') THEN 'Bracketed Retention' WHEN retention_logic IN ('strict','day_n','exact_day') THEN 'Strict Day-N Retention' ELSE 'Other' END
tags: retention; cohort; loyalty; user-lifecycle; repeat-usage; cohort-analysis; customer-analytics
industry: Retail; E-commerce; SaaS; Healthcare; Education; Finance; Media; Technology; Manufacturing; Other
w3_data_layer: {"event":"retention_measurement","retention":{"type":"Classic Retention","logic":"user returned in exact analysis period based on cohort start date","cohort_date":"2026-04-01","return_window":"day_7"}}
sql_query:
WITH user_activity AS (
  SELECT
    user_id,
    DATE(MIN(event_timestamp)) AS cohort_date,
    DATE(event_timestamp) AS activity_date
  FROM raw_events
  GROUP BY 1,3
), retention_base AS (
  SELECT
    user_id,
    cohort_date,
    activity_date,
    DATE_DIFF(activity_date, cohort_date, DAY) AS days_since_cohort
  FROM user_activity
)
SELECT
  user_id,
  cohort_date,
  CASE
    WHEN days_since_cohort = 7 THEN 'Strict Day-N Retention'
    WHEN days_since_cohort >= 7 THEN 'Rolling Retention'
    WHEN days_since_cohort BETWEEN 7 AND 13 THEN 'Bracketed Retention'
    ELSE 'Classic Retention'
  END AS retention_type
FROM retention_base
WHERE days_since_cohort >= 0;
calculation_notes: Define and govern allowed values centrally because retention type is often a reporting configuration rather than a raw behavioral attribute. Best practice is to populate this dimension in the semantic layer, BI model, or cohort output table rather than on every digital event unless the product explicitly emits retention methodology. Ensure the same cohort start rule is used across all retention types, such as first_seen_date, signup_date, first_purchase_date, or first_key_action_date. Strict Day-N counts return only on the exact day or period. Rolling retention counts return on or after the target day. Bracketed retention counts return within a defined range such as days 7-13. Classic retention is often used as a generic label for exact-period cohort retention in standard dashboards. Do not mix user-based and account-based retention definitions in the same field. Capture timezone consistently when deriving cohort and return dates.
business_use_case: A growth team compares D7 retention for a mobile app using Strict Day-N Retention versus Rolling Retention to understand whether users are returning exactly on day 7 or simply returning any time after day 7. Finance and product teams then align on one retention type for board reporting to avoid conflicting KPI values.
source_data: Digital Analytics; Business Intelligence; CRM; Product Analytics; Data Warehouse
dependencies: User ID; Cohort date; Return date; Retained users metric; Active users metric; Signup event or first key action event; Retention window definition
report_attributes: Dimensions: Retention type, Cohort date, Return window, User segment; Metrics: Retained users, Retention rate, Active users, Churn rate; KPIs: D1 retention, D7 retention, D30 retention, Repeat purchase rate
related_dimensions: Cohort date;Retention window;User status;Customer lifecycle stage;Acquisition channel;First purchase date
derived_dimensions: Retention rate band;Retention methodology group;Cohort performance tier
aliases: retention methodology; cohort retention type; retention logic; retention model

name: Registration type
description: Categorises the method, channel, or account pathway a user used to complete registration or sign-up. Typical values include email, social login, SSO, phone, guest-to-account, referral invite, in-app registration, and offline-assisted registration. This dimension is used to compare conversion quality, onboarding performance, and downstream retention by registration pathway.
formula: Derived from the registration submission context using a prioritised mapping of identity method and registration source. Example logic: CASE WHEN sso_provider IS NOT NULL THEN CONCAT('sso:', LOWER(sso_provider)) WHEN social_provider IS NOT NULL THEN CONCAT('social:', LOWER(social_provider)) WHEN phone_verified = TRUE THEN 'phone' WHEN guest_conversion = TRUE THEN 'guest-to-account' WHEN invite_code IS NOT NULL THEN 'invite' WHEN registration_channel = 'offline' THEN 'offline-assisted' ELSE 'email' END
tags: registration; signup; account creation; identity method; conversion; onboarding; user acquisition
industry: Retail; E-commerce; SaaS; Healthcare; Education; Finance; Media; Technology; Other
aggregation_window: Event, Session, User, Time based - Daily/Monthly/Yearly
sql_query:
SELECT
  user_id,
  event_timestamp,
  CASE
    WHEN LOWER(COALESCE(sso_provider, '')) <> '' THEN CONCAT('sso:', LOWER(sso_provider))
    WHEN LOWER(COALESCE(social_provider, '')) <> '' THEN CONCAT('social:', LOWER(social_provider))
    WHEN COALESCE(phone_verified, FALSE) = TRUE THEN 'phone'
    WHEN COALESCE(guest_conversion, FALSE) = TRUE THEN 'guest-to-account'
    WHEN invite_code IS NOT NULL THEN 'invite'
    WHEN LOWER(COALESCE(registration_channel, '')) = 'offline' THEN 'offline-assisted'
    ELSE 'email'
  END AS registration_type
FROM raw_events
WHERE event_name IN ('sign_up','complete_registration','account_created');
calculation_notes: Capture on the definitive registration completion event, not on form start. Standardise values to a controlled taxonomy and lowercase format. Avoid mixing provider and method unless intentionally designed, e.g. social:google vs sso:okta. Persist as a user-scoped property after first successful registration for longitudinal analysis, but also retain event-scoped value on the registration event for auditability. If a user retries multiple methods before success, only store the successful method on the completion event. Do not populate with raw email domains or identifiers. Align guest-to-account logic with commerce account merge rules.
business_use_case: Growth and product teams compare conversion rate, activation rate, and 90-day retention for users who registered via email versus social:google versus sso:okta to determine which registration pathways drive the highest quality users and where onboarding friction exists.
source_data: Digital Analytics, CRM, Identity Provider, Business Intelligence
dependencies: sign_up event;complete_registration event;user_id;identity provider metadata;guest conversion flag;invite code flag;phone verification status
report_attributes: Dimensions: Registration type, Registration provider, Device category, Source/Medium, Landing page; Metrics: Registrations, Registration conversion rate, Activation rate, Revenue per registered user; KPIs: Cost per registration, Retention rate
dashboard_usage: Executive KPI;Acquisition Performance;Registration Funnel;Onboarding Analysis;Identity and Access Reporting
related_dimensions: Registration provider;Registration method;Authentication type;User type;Acquisition channel;Device category
derived_dimensions: Registration type group;Self-serve vs assisted registration;Federated vs native registration;Registration channel family
aliases: signup type; account creation type; registration method; sign-up method; identity signup type

name: product finding method
description: Categorises the primary method a user used to discover or arrive at a product detail page, product listing, or selected product within the digital experience. Typical values include site search, category navigation, homepage promotion, recommendation widget, internal link, external campaign landing, filter/sort refinement, wishlist, recently viewed, and direct product URL. This dimension helps explain product discovery behavior prior to engagement or conversion.
formula: Derived from the first qualifying product discovery interaction within the session or the interaction immediately preceding product_view/select_item, using event context and page/module metadata. Example logic: CASE WHEN event_name IN ('view_search_results','search') OR search_term IS NOT NULL THEN 'site_search' WHEN list_name ILIKE '%recommend%' OR module_type IN ('recommendation','personalization') THEN 'recommendation' WHEN page_type='category' OR item_list_name ILIKE '%category%' THEN 'category_navigation' WHEN page_type='home' AND module_type IN ('hero','promo','featured') THEN 'homepage_promotion' WHEN page_type='wishlist' THEN 'wishlist' WHEN page_type='recently_viewed' THEN 'recently_viewed' WHEN page_referrer_type='internal' AND page_type='product' THEN 'internal_link' WHEN traffic_medium IS NOT NULL AND landing_page_type='product' THEN 'external_landing' WHEN filter_applied=true OR sort_applied=true THEN 'filter_or_sort' WHEN page_location ILIKE '%/product/%' AND entrance=1 THEN 'direct_product_url' ELSE 'other' END
tags: product discovery; site search; navigation; merchandising; product analytics; e-commerce; onsite behavior; attribution
aggregation_window: Event, Session, User, Time based - Daily/Weekly/Monthly/Quarterly/Yearly
sql_query:
WITH product_context AS (
  SELECT
    user_pseudo_id,
    session_id,
    event_timestamp,
    event_name,
    MAX(CASE WHEN ep.key = 'product_finding_method' THEN ep.value.string_value END) AS explicit_method,
    MAX(CASE WHEN ep.key = 'search_term' THEN ep.value.string_value END) AS search_term,
    MAX(CASE WHEN ep.key = 'page_type' THEN ep.value.string_value END) AS page_type,
    MAX(CASE WHEN ep.key = 'module_type' THEN ep.value.string_value END) AS module_type,
    MAX(CASE WHEN ep.key = 'item_list_name' THEN ep.value.string_value END) AS item_list_name,
    MAX(CASE WHEN ep.key = 'landing_page_type' THEN ep.value.string_value END) AS landing_page_type,
    MAX(CASE WHEN ep.key = 'referrer_type' THEN ep.value.string_value END) AS referrer_type,
    MAX(CASE WHEN ep.key = 'filter_applied' THEN ep.value.string_value END) AS filter_applied,
    MAX(CASE WHEN ep.key = 'sort_applied' THEN ep.value.string_value END) AS sort_applied,
    MAX(CASE WHEN ep.key = 'entrance' THEN ep.value.int_value END) AS entrance
  FROM `project.dataset.events_*` e,
  UNNEST(e.event_params) ep
  GROUP BY 1,2,3,4
)
SELECT
  user_pseudo_id,
  session_id,
  event_timestamp,
  COALESCE(
    explicit_method,
    CASE
      WHEN event_name IN ('search','view_search_results') OR search_term IS NOT NULL THEN 'site_search'
      WHEN LOWER(COALESCE(item_list_name,'')) LIKE '%recommend%' OR module_type IN ('recommendation','personalization') THEN 'recommendation'
      WHEN page_type = 'category' OR LOWER(COALESCE(item_list_name,'')) LIKE '%category%' THEN 'category_navigation'
      WHEN page_type = 'home' AND module_type IN ('hero','promo','featured') THEN 'homepage_promotion'
      WHEN page_type = 'wishlist' THEN 'wishlist'
      WHEN page_type = 'recently_viewed' THEN 'recently_viewed'
      WHEN referrer_type = 'internal' AND page_type = 'product' THEN 'internal_link'
      WHEN landing_page_type = 'product' THEN 'external_landing'
      WHEN filter_applied = 'true' OR sort_applied = 'true' THEN 'filter_or_sort'
      WHEN entrance = 1 AND page_type = 'product' THEN 'direct_product_url'
      ELSE 'other'
    END
  ) AS product_finding_method
FROM product_context;
calculation_notes: Capture at the moment a product is selected or viewed, not only on add_to_cart, to preserve discovery attribution. Prefer explicit tagging from UI components over inferred logic. Standardise allowed values in a controlled taxonomy and avoid free-text values. If multiple discovery actions occur in a session, define whether this dimension represents first-touch product discovery, last-touch before product view, or session-primary method. For list-based interactions, pass item_list_name/item_list_id consistently. For search, include search_term and search results context. For recommendation modules, distinguish algorithmic recommendations from manual promotions if needed. Ensure direct product URL is only assigned for true entrances to PDPs. In SPAs, update the value on route change and product selection events.
business_use_case: Merchandising and UX teams use this dimension to compare conversion rate, add-to-cart rate, and revenue by discovery path, such as determining whether products found via site search convert better than products found via recommendation widgets or category navigation.
dependencies: product_view/select_item event; page_type; item_list_name or item_list_id; search_term; module_type; referrer_type; landing_page_type; session_id
report_attributes: Dimensions: Product Finding Method, Item List Name, Search Term, Page Type, Module Type; Metrics: Product Views, Select Item, Add to Cart, Conversion Rate, Revenue per Session; KPIs: PDP Conversion Rate, Search-assisted Revenue
dashboard_usage: Merchandising Performance;Product Discovery Analysis;Site Search Dashboard;Conversion Funnel;Executive E-commerce Overview
related_dimensions: item_list_name;search term;page type;module type;traffic source;landing page type;referrer type
derived_dimensions: product discovery channel group;search-assisted product discovery flag;recommendation-assisted discovery flag;primary product discovery path
aliases: product discovery method; product discovery source; find method; item discovery method; product entry method

name: Browse Category
description: The product or content category a user is browsing at the time of a category listing, collection, taxonomy, or navigation page view. It categorises browsing activity into a standardized hierarchy such as Electronics, Women > Shoes, or Blog > Analytics, enabling analysis of demand, engagement, and downstream conversion by category.
formula: Derived from the page context, product taxonomy, or navigation metadata. Typical logic: use explicit category value from data layer if present; else map page_type='category' and category_id/category_name from CMS or commerce platform; else infer from URL path taxonomy. Standardized formula example: COALESCE(data_layer.category_name, commerce.category_name, cms.section_name, taxonomy_map.category_from_url).
tags: category; browse; product taxonomy; site navigation; merchandising; content grouping; e-commerce; listing page
aggregation_window: Event, Session, User, Time based - Daily/Weekly/Monthly/Yearly
sql_query:
SELECT
  event_date,
  user_pseudo_id,
  session_id,
  event_name,
  COALESCE(
    (SELECT ep.value.string_value FROM UNNEST(event_params) ep WHERE ep.key = 'browse_category'),
    (SELECT ep.value.string_value FROM UNNEST(event_params) ep WHERE ep.key = 'item_list_name'),
    (SELECT ep.value.string_value FROM UNNEST(event_params) ep WHERE ep.key = 'item_category'),
    page_category
  ) AS browse_category
FROM raw_ga4_events
WHERE event_name IN ('view_item_list','page_view','category_view');
calculation_notes: Capture on category/listing page views and optionally persist at session level for pathing analysis. Use a controlled taxonomy and avoid free-text category names. If hierarchical categories exist, define a canonical delimiter such as ' > '. Distinguish browse category from product category on PDPs. For multi-category products, use the page/list context rather than product master category. Ensure URL-derived fallback mappings are maintained as site taxonomy changes.
business_use_case: Merchandising and analytics teams use Browse Category to compare sessions, product list views, add-to-cart rate, and revenue for categories like Women > Shoes versus Men > Accessories, helping prioritize promotions, navigation changes, and inventory placement.
source_data: Digital Analytics, Business Intelligence, CMS, E-commerce Platform
dependencies: page_view event; category_view event; view_item_list event; page_type dimension; product/category taxonomy mapping
report_attributes: Dimensions: Browse Category, Page Type, Device Category, Traffic Source; Metrics: Sessions, Users, Product List Views, Add-to-Carts, Conversion Rate, Revenue; KPIs: Category CVR, Revenue per Session
related_dimensions: Product Category;Page Type;Site Section;Item List Name;Navigation Path
derived_dimensions: Browse Category Level 1;Browse Category Level 2;Category Group;Top Browse Category Flag
aliases: Category Browsed; Listing Category; Site Category; Product List Category; Collection Name

name: Payment Method
description: Categorises the tender or funding instrument selected or used to complete a transaction, such as credit card, debit card, PayPal, Apple Pay, bank transfer, gift card, buy now pay later, or cash on delivery. This dimension is used to analyse conversion, revenue mix, checkout behaviour, payment performance, and customer preferences by payment type.
formula: If explicitly captured at checkout or purchase, use the submitted payment_type value. If multiple raw gateway values exist, map them to a governed taxonomy using rules such as: CASE WHEN lower(raw_payment_method) IN ('visa','mastercard','amex','discover','credit_card') THEN 'Credit Card' WHEN lower(raw_payment_method) IN ('debit_card','maestro') THEN 'Debit Card' WHEN lower(raw_payment_method) IN ('paypal','paypal_express') THEN 'PayPal' WHEN lower(raw_payment_method) IN ('apple_pay','applepay') THEN 'Apple Pay' WHEN lower(raw_payment_method) IN ('google_pay','gpay') THEN 'Google Pay' WHEN lower(raw_payment_method) IN ('klarna','afterpay','affirm','bnpl') THEN 'Buy Now Pay Later' WHEN lower(raw_payment_method) IN ('bank_transfer','ach','sepa') THEN 'Bank Transfer' WHEN lower(raw_payment_method) IN ('gift_card','store_credit') THEN 'Gift Card / Store Credit' WHEN lower(raw_payment_method) IN ('cash_on_delivery','cod') THEN 'Cash on Delivery' ELSE 'Other / Unknown' END
tags: payments; checkout; purchase; transaction; revenue; conversion; payment gateway; ecommerce
aggregation_window: Event, Session, User, Time based - Daily/Weekly/Monthly/Yearly
sql_query:
SELECT
  event_date,
  event_timestamp,
  user_pseudo_id,
  event_name,
  ecommerce.transaction_id AS transaction_id,
  COALESCE(
    (SELECT ep.value.string_value FROM UNNEST(event_params) ep WHERE ep.key = 'payment_type'),
    JSON_VALUE(event_json, '$.ecommerce.payment_method'),
    payment_method,
    'Other / Unknown'
  ) AS payment_method
FROM analytics_raw.ga4_events
WHERE event_name IN ('add_payment_info','purchase');
calculation_notes: Capture the selected payment method at add_payment_info and persist the final authorised payment method on purchase. Use a controlled taxonomy to standardise gateway-specific values. Distinguish payment method from card brand where possible; card brand can be stored as a separate related dimension. For split tenders, either store the primary payment method for analytics simplicity or implement a multi-value companion field in BI. Ensure failed payment attempts are tracked separately from successful purchase events to support payment failure analysis. Do not store full card numbers or sensitive authentication data. If payment method changes during checkout, the purchase event should reflect the final method used.
business_use_case: An e-commerce team analyses conversion rate, average order value, and payment failure rate by Payment Method to determine whether digital wallets outperform cards on mobile and whether BNPL drives higher basket sizes.
source_data: Digital Analytics; Payment Gateway; ERP; Business Intelligence
dependencies: add_payment_info event; purchase event; transaction_id; revenue metric; checkout funnel steps; payment gateway response
report_attributes: Dimensions: Payment Method, Device Category, Country, Checkout Step; Metrics: Purchases, Revenue, Conversion Rate, Payment Failure Rate, Average Order Value; KPIs: Checkout Completion Rate, Revenue Share by Payment Method
dashboard_usage: E-commerce Performance;Checkout Funnel;Revenue Analysis;Payments Operations;Executive Summary
related_dimensions: Card Brand;Payment Status;Checkout Step;Transaction ID;Currency;Device Category
derived_dimensions: Payment Method Group;Digital Wallet vs Card;Primary Payment Method;Payment Method Revenue Share
aliases: payment type; payment tender; tender type; transaction payment method; checkout payment method

name: Order ID
description: Unique identifier assigned to a completed order or transaction. This dimension categorises conversion records by the specific purchase instance and is used to join digital analytics data with commerce, ERP, CRM, and fulfillment systems for order-level analysis, reconciliation, and deduplication.
formula: Typically sourced directly from the commerce or order management system at the moment an order is created or confirmed. If derived, use the canonical transaction identifier from the backend order table, e.g. COALESCE(order_id, transaction_id, purchase_id).
tags: order; transaction; purchase; conversion; ecommerce; checkout; identifier; attribution; reconciliation
industry: Retail; E-commerce; SaaS; Finance; Technology; Other
aggregation_window: Event, Session, User, Time based - Daily/Monthly/Yearly
sql_query: SELECT event_date, event_timestamp, user_pseudo_id, event_name, (SELECT ep.value.string_value FROM UNNEST(event_params) ep WHERE ep.key = 'transaction_id') AS order_id FROM `project.dataset.events_*` WHERE event_name = 'purchase' AND (SELECT ep.value.string_value FROM UNNEST(event_params) ep WHERE ep.key = 'transaction_id') IS NOT NULL;
calculation_notes: Capture only on the definitive conversion event, typically purchase or order confirmation, after the backend has successfully created the order. The value must be stable, unique, and identical across analytics, commerce platform, ERP, and finance systems. Never generate this solely on the client if a backend canonical ID exists. Use a string type to preserve leading zeros and alphanumeric formats. Ensure retries, duplicate page loads, and server/client dual tagging do not create duplicate purchase events with the same Order ID unless deduplication logic is in place. In GA4, transaction_id is critical for purchase deduplication. Avoid reusing IDs for test orders, partial payments, or split shipments unless business rules explicitly define them as the same order.
business_use_case: An e-commerce analytics team filters revenue by Order ID to investigate a customer complaint, validate whether a purchase event fired correctly, reconcile analytics revenue against ERP records, and identify duplicate transactions inflating conversion reporting.
source_data: Digital Analytics, Business Intelligence, ERP, CRM, Order Management System, Payment Platform
dependencies: Purchase event;Checkout completion;Revenue metric;Currency;Product line items;Order confirmation page or server-side purchase event
report_attributes: Dimensions: Order ID, Transaction ID, Purchase Event, Order Date; Metrics: Orders, Transactions, Revenue, Average Order Value, Items Purchased; KPIs: Conversion Rate, Revenue per User
dashboard_usage: E-commerce Performance;Conversion Analysis;Revenue Reconciliation;Order Operations;Executive Revenue Summary
related_dimensions: Order Status;Payment Method;Coupon Code;Currency;Product SKU;Customer ID;Transaction Date;Store ID
derived_dimensions: First Order ID;Repeat Order Flag;Order Sequence Number;Order Value Band;Order Recency Bucket
aliases: Transaction ID; Purchase ID; Sales Order ID; Order Number; transaction_id

name: Tracking Code
description: A string-based identifier attached to inbound links, campaigns, creatives, affiliates, or internal marketing placements to classify traffic and downstream user behavior by the exact code used for attribution, routing, or campaign governance. It categorises data by the specific tracking value passed in the URL, redirect, tag manager, or data layer.
formula: Typically captured directly from URL query parameters or redirect metadata. Example derivation: COALESCE(url_parameter('tracking_code'), url_parameter('cid'), url_parameter('utm_id'), event_parameter('tracking_code'), user_property('tracking_code')).
tags: campaign tracking; attribution; marketing; url parameters; traffic source; campaign code; tagging; acquisition
industry: Retail; E-commerce; SaaS; Healthcare; Education; Finance; Media; Technology; Manufacturing; Other
aggregation_window: Event, Session, User, Time based - Hourly/Daily/Monthly/Yearly
sql_query:
SELECT
  event_date,
  user_pseudo_id,
  session_id,
  COALESCE(
    (SELECT ep.value.string_value FROM UNNEST(event_params) ep WHERE ep.key = 'tracking_code'),
    REGEXP_EXTRACT(page_location, r'(?i)[?&]tracking_code=([^&#]+)'),
    REGEXP_EXTRACT(page_location, r'(?i)[?&]cid=([^&#]+)'),
    (SELECT ep.value.string_value FROM UNNEST(event_params) ep WHERE ep.key = 'utm_id')
  ) AS tracking_code
FROM `project.dataset.events_*`
WHERE event_name IN ('page_view','session_start','purchase','generate_lead');
calculation_notes: Capture on landing page and persist for the full session, and optionally to user scope for multi-session attribution analysis. Standardize casing, trim whitespace, and decode URL-encoded values before storage. Define a clear precedence order when multiple campaign identifiers are present. Exclude internal QA/test codes or flag them separately. If the code changes mid-session, decide whether to keep first-touch session value, last-touch event value, or both. Ensure redirects preserve the parameter. Avoid using values that contain PII such as email addresses or customer IDs.
business_use_case: Marketing teams filter sessions, conversions, and revenue by tracking code to evaluate the exact performance of a campaign link such as SPRING24_EMAIL_A1 versus SPRING24_EMAIL_B2, enabling granular creative, partner, or placement optimization.
source_data: Digital Analytics, Business Intelligence, CRM, Ad Platforms
dependencies: Landing Page URL; Session ID; Campaign Parameters; Traffic Source; Page View Event; Session Start Event
report_attributes: Dimensions: Tracking Code, Session Source, Session Medium, Campaign, Landing Page; Metrics: Sessions, Users, Conversions, Revenue, Engagement Rate; KPIs: ROAS, Conversion Rate, CPA
derived_dimensions: Tracking Code Group; Tracking Code Type; Campaign Family; Channel Mapping
aliases: campaign code; cid; utm_id; promo code; source code; marketing code

name: products
description: A product-level dimension that identifies the item or set of items associated with an ecommerce interaction such as product view, add to cart, checkout, purchase, refund, or promotion click. It categorises behavioral and commercial data by product so analysts can break down performance by SKU, product name, variant, or catalog item captured on the event.
formula: Derived from the ecommerce items array captured on product-related events. At event level, products = item_name when a single item exists; when multiple items exist, products = concatenated list of item_name values ordered by item index. Preferred implementation is to persist each item as a separate unnested row for product-level analysis.
tags: product; ecommerce; item; sku; merchandising; catalog; purchase; cart; product-analysis
aggregation_window: Event, Session, User, Time based - Daily/Weekly/Monthly/Yearly
sql_query:
SELECT
  event_date,
  event_timestamp,
  user_pseudo_id,
  event_name,
  item.item_id AS product_id,
  item.item_name AS products,
  item.item_brand,
  item.item_category,
  item.item_variant,
  item.price,
  item.quantity
FROM `project.dataset.events_*`,
UNNEST(items) AS item
WHERE event_name IN ('view_item','add_to_cart','begin_checkout','purchase','refund');
calculation_notes: Capture this dimension on every ecommerce event using a consistent product catalog key and human-readable product name. For GA4, use the items array rather than a custom event parameter whenever possible. In Adobe, the products variable is the canonical product string for merchandising and product reports; optionally mirror product name or SKU into an eVar for easier pathing and classification. Avoid mixing SKU and product name in the same field. If multiple products are present on one event, store each item separately in the warehouse to prevent inflated counts and ambiguous attribution. Ensure product naming is stable over time or maintain a product master lookup for historical consistency. If using bundles or kits, define whether the parent bundle, child items, or both should populate the dimension.
business_use_case: Merchandising and growth teams use this dimension to identify which products drive the highest product views, add-to-cart rate, checkout starts, purchases, revenue, refund rate, and conversion efficiency. Example: filter purchase and add_to_cart events by products = 'Classic Running Shoes' to compare PDP engagement, cart rate, and revenue contribution versus other footwear items.

name: Pages
description: Identifies the page or screen viewed by a user and categorises interaction data by content location. Typically represented as the page path, canonical URL, or page name, this dimension is used to analyze content consumption, navigation behavior, landing/exit patterns, and page-level performance across sessions and users.
formula: If page metadata is available, derive as COALESCE(canonical_page_path, page_path, page_location_path, page_title). For web analytics implementations, commonly extracted from the page_view event using the URL path component of page_location or a mapped page_name value.
tags: page; page path; page name; content; site navigation; landing page; exit page; content analytics; web analytics
industry: Retail; E-commerce; SaaS; Healthcare; Education; Finance; Media; Technology; Manufacturing; Other
aggregation_window: Event, Session, User, Time based - Hourly/Daily/Monthly/Yearly
ga4_event: page_location, page_title, page_referrer (derived page path from page_location on page_view event)
sql_query:
SELECT
  event_date,
  user_pseudo_id,
  session_id,
  event_timestamp,
  COALESCE(
    REGEXP_EXTRACT((SELECT ep.value.string_value FROM UNNEST(event_params) ep WHERE ep.key = 'page_location'), r'^https?://[^/]+(/[^?#]*)'),
    (SELECT ep.value.string_value FROM UNNEST(event_params) ep WHERE ep.key = 'page_path'),
    (SELECT ep.value.string_value FROM UNNEST(event_params) ep WHERE ep.key = 'page_title')
  ) AS pages
FROM `project.dataset.events_*`
WHERE event_name = 'page_view';
calculation_notes: Capture on every page_view event after page metadata is fully available in the browser or application state. Standardize whether the value represents page path, full URL, or business-friendly page name, and keep that choice consistent across reporting. Remove query strings and fragments unless they are intentionally part of page identity. For SPAs, fire virtual page_view events on route changes. Align canonicalization rules across GA4, Adobe, and warehouse models to avoid duplicate page values such as '/pricing' vs '/pricing/'.
business_use_case: Content, product, and marketing teams use Pages to identify which pages drive the most engagement, where users drop off in conversion funnels, which landing pages attract qualified traffic, and which support or help pages correlate with reduced support tickets.
source_data: Digital Analytics; Business Intelligence; CMS metadata

report_attributes: Dimensions: Page path, Page title, Landing page, Exit page, Content group; Metrics: Views, Users, Sessions, Engagement rate, Conversions, Revenue; KPIs: Top pages, Landing page conversion rate, Exit rate

name: Site Sections
description: Categorises website or app content into logical sections based on page location, navigation hierarchy, content taxonomy, or business-defined grouping. Typical examples include Home, Product Listing, Product Detail, Blog, Support, Checkout, Account, and Help Center. This dimension is used to analyse engagement, conversion, and navigation performance by major area of the digital property.
formula: CASE WHEN page_type IS NOT NULL THEN page_type WHEN content_group IS NOT NULL THEN content_group WHEN page_path LIKE '/products/%' THEN 'Product' WHEN page_path LIKE '/category/%' THEN 'Category' WHEN page_path LIKE '/blog/%' THEN 'Blog' WHEN page_path LIKE '/support/%' THEN 'Support' WHEN page_path LIKE '/checkout%' THEN 'Checkout' WHEN page_path LIKE '/account/%' THEN 'Account' WHEN page_path IN ('/','/home') THEN 'Home' ELSE 'Other' END
tags: content grouping; site taxonomy; navigation; page categorisation; content analysis; journey analysis
industry: Retail; E-commerce; SaaS; Healthcare; Education; Finance; Media; Technology; Manufacturing; Other
aggregation_window: Event, Session, User, Time based - Hourly/Daily/Monthly/Yearly
sql_query:
SELECT
  event_date,
  user_pseudo_id,
  session_id,
  event_timestamp,
  COALESCE(
    (SELECT ep.value.string_value FROM UNNEST(event_params) ep WHERE ep.key = 'site_section'),
    CASE
      WHEN (SELECT ep.value.string_value FROM UNNEST(event_params) ep WHERE ep.key = 'page_type') IS NOT NULL
        THEN (SELECT ep.value.string_value FROM UNNEST(event_params) ep WHERE ep.key = 'page_type')
      WHEN (SELECT ep.value.string_value FROM UNNEST(event_params) ep WHERE ep.key = 'page_location') LIKE '%/products/%' THEN 'Product'
      WHEN (SELECT ep.value.string_value FROM UNNEST(event_params) ep WHERE ep.key = 'page_location') LIKE '%/category/%' THEN 'Category'
      WHEN (SELECT ep.value.string_value FROM UNNEST(event_params) ep WHERE ep.key = 'page_location') LIKE '%/blog/%' THEN 'Blog'
      WHEN (SELECT ep.value.string_value FROM UNNEST(event_params) ep WHERE ep.key = 'page_location') LIKE '%/support/%' THEN 'Support'
      WHEN (SELECT ep.value.string_value FROM UNNEST(event_params) ep WHERE ep.key = 'page_location') LIKE '%/checkout%' THEN 'Checkout'
      WHEN REGEXP_EXTRACT((SELECT ep.value.string_value FROM UNNEST(event_params) ep WHERE ep.key = 'page_location'), r'https?://[^/]+(/.*)') IN ('/','/home') THEN 'Home'
      ELSE 'Other'
    END
  ) AS site_section
FROM `project.dataset.events_*`
WHERE event_name = 'page_view';
calculation_notes: Prefer explicit tagging from CMS or data layer over URL-rule inference because URL structures change over time. Capture on every page_view and virtual page_view event. Standardise values with a controlled taxonomy and naming convention. Avoid mixing page template names with business sections unless intentionally aligned. For SPAs, update this value on route change. If multiple taxonomies exist, define precedence rules between site_section, content_group, and page_type. Maintain a lookup table for historical consistency when site IA changes.
business_use_case: Marketing and product teams use Site Sections to compare engagement and conversion rates between Blog, Product Detail, and Checkout sections, identify where users drop off in journeys, and determine which content areas drive the most qualified traffic and revenue.
dependencies: page_view event; page_location; page_title; content_group; page_type; CMS taxonomy mapping
report_attributes: Dimensions: Site Sections, Page path, Page title, Content group, Landing page; Metrics: Views, Users, Sessions, Engagement rate, Conversions, Revenue; KPIs: Conversion rate, Bounce rate, Avg engagement time

name: Sub Section 1
description: Categorises content, page, screen, product, or experience interactions into a first-level subsection within a broader site section or information architecture. This dimension is used to group user activity under a meaningful mid-level navigation or taxonomy bucket, enabling analysis of engagement, conversion, and performance by subsection rather than by individual page or event.
formula: Derived from page path, screen name, content taxonomy, or CMS metadata. Example logic: CASE WHEN page_path LIKE '/products/%' THEN SPLIT_PART(page_path,'/',3) WHEN content_sub_section IS NOT NULL THEN content_sub_section ELSE '(not set)' END
tags: content taxonomy; site structure; navigation; content grouping; page classification; subsection; information architecture
industry: Retail; E-commerce; SaaS; Healthcare; Education; Finance; Media; Technology; Manufacturing; Other

sql_query:
SELECT

  event_timestamp,
  COALESCE(
    (SELECT ep.value.string_value FROM UNNEST(event_params) ep WHERE ep.key = 'sub_section_1'),
    REGEXP_EXTRACT((SELECT ep.value.string_value FROM UNNEST(event_params) ep WHERE ep.key = 'page_location'), r'^https?://[^/]+/[^/]+/([^/?#]+)'),

calculation_notes: Populate at the time of page_view, screen_view, and any key content interaction events so the value persists across downstream analysis. Prefer CMS-driven taxonomy over URL parsing because URLs can change and may not reflect business structure. Standardise casing and naming conventions, such as title case or lowercase, to avoid duplicate values. If using SPA frameworks, ensure the dimension is refreshed on virtual page changes. For Adobe, use an eVar with visit or hit expiration depending on reporting needs; hit-level is recommended when subsection can change frequently within a session. Avoid sending free-form values from user input. Define a fallback value such as '(not set)' only when taxonomy is unavailable.
business_use_case: A content strategy team filters engagement and conversion reports by Sub Section 1 to compare how Blog, Documentation, Pricing, and Support subsections contribute to lead generation, product adoption, or revenue. An e-commerce team uses it to compare performance of Men, Women, Kids, and Sale subsections within a broader catalog section.

name: Sub Section 2
description: Content hierarchy dimension representing the second-level subsection or nested content grouping within a page, screen, article, product detail experience, help center, or application information architecture. It categorises user interactions by the secondary section label that sits beneath the primary section, enabling analysis of navigation depth, content performance, and engagement by sub-area of the digital property.
formula: Derived from page or screen taxonomy. Typical logic: if explicit subsection value exists in data layer, use that; else parse from content hierarchy metadata or URL path. Example derivation: COALESCE(data_layer.sub_section_2, cms.content_hierarchy.level_2, SPLIT_PART(TRIM(BOTH '/' FROM page_path), '/', 2), '(not set)').
tags: content taxonomy; site structure; navigation; content grouping; subsection; hierarchy; page metadata; reporting dimension
industry: Retail; E-commerce; SaaS; Healthcare; Education; Finance; Media; Technology; Manufacturing; Other

sql_query:
SELECT
  event_date,
  user_pseudo_id,

  COALESCE(
    (SELECT ep.value.string_value FROM UNNEST(event_params) ep WHERE ep.key = 'sub_section_2'),
    JSON_VALUE(event_json, '$.page.subSection2'),
    SPLIT(REGEXP_REPLACE((SELECT ep.value.string_value FROM UNNEST(event_params) ep WHERE ep.key = 'page_path'), r'^/|/$', ''), '/')[SAFE_OFFSET(1)],
    '(not set)'
  ) AS sub_section_2
FROM `project.dataset.events_*`
WHERE event_name IN ('page_view','screen_view','content_view');
calculation_notes: Capture on every page_view, screen_view, and key content interaction event to preserve attribution when users continue interacting without another page load. Prefer CMS-driven taxonomy over URL parsing because URLs often change and may not reflect business hierarchy. Standardise casing, spacing, and spelling through a controlled vocabulary. Use '(not set)' rather than null for reporting consistency. If the property has fewer than two hierarchy levels, populate a fallback value such as 'none' or '(not applicable)' based on governance rules. In SPAs, refresh this value on route change before firing analytics events. Ensure Adobe eVar persistence settings align with intended attribution window if used beyond hit-level reporting.
business_use_case: A content strategy team filters engagement and conversion reports by Sub Section 2 to compare how users behave in secondary areas such as 'Laptops', 'Savings Accounts', 'Admissions Requirements', or 'Support Articles'. This helps identify which sub-areas drive deeper engagement, higher conversion rates, or increased exits and informs navigation, merchandising, and editorial prioritisation.

name: page type
description: Categorises a page or screen into a standardized content or functional type based on its purpose in the user journey, such as home, category, product detail, article, search results, cart, checkout, account, or support. This dimension enables consistent analysis of engagement, conversion, and navigation performance across templates and experiences.
formula: CASE WHEN page_template IS NOT NULL THEN page_template WHEN page_url LIKE '%/product/%' OR content_group = 'pdp' THEN 'product-detail' WHEN page_url LIKE '%/category/%' OR content_group = 'plp' THEN 'category-listing' WHEN page_url LIKE '%/search%' THEN 'search-results' WHEN page_url LIKE '%/cart%' THEN 'cart' WHEN page_url LIKE '%/checkout%' THEN 'checkout' WHEN page_url IN ('/','/home') OR page_name = 'home' THEN 'home' WHEN page_url LIKE '%/blog/%' OR content_type = 'article' THEN 'article' WHEN page_url LIKE '%/account%' THEN 'account' WHEN page_url LIKE '%/support%' OR page_url LIKE '%/help%' THEN 'support' ELSE 'other' END
tags: content classification; page taxonomy; site structure; content grouping; navigation; template analysis
industry: Retail; E-commerce; SaaS; Healthcare; Education; Finance; Media; Technology; Manufacturing; Other
aggregation_window: Event, Session, User, Time based - Hourly/Daily/Monthly/Yearly
sql_query: SELECT event_date, user_pseudo_id, session_id, event_timestamp, COALESCE((SELECT ep.value.string_value FROM UNNEST(event_params) ep WHERE ep.key = 'page_type'), CASE WHEN (SELECT ep.value.string_value FROM UNNEST(event_params) ep WHERE ep.key = 'page_template') IS NOT NULL THEN (SELECT ep.value.string_value FROM UNNEST(event_params) ep WHERE ep.key = 'page_template') WHEN (SELECT ep.value.string_value FROM UNNEST(event_params) ep WHERE ep.key = 'page_location') LIKE '%/product/%' THEN 'product-detail' WHEN (SELECT ep.value.string_value FROM UNNEST(event_params) ep WHERE ep.key = 'page_location') LIKE '%/category/%' THEN 'category-listing' WHEN (SELECT ep.value.string_value FROM UNNEST(event_params) ep WHERE ep.key = 'page_location') LIKE '%/search%' THEN 'search-results' WHEN (SELECT ep.value.string_value FROM UNNEST(event_params) ep WHERE ep.key = 'page_location') LIKE '%/cart%' THEN 'cart' WHEN (SELECT ep.value.string_value FROM UNNEST(event_params) ep WHERE ep.key = 'page_location') LIKE '%/checkout%' THEN 'checkout' WHEN (SELECT ep.value.string_value FROM UNNEST(event_params) ep WHERE ep.key = 'page_location') IN ('/','/home') THEN 'home' ELSE 'other' END) AS page_type FROM `project.dataset.events_*` WHERE event_name = 'page_view';
calculation_notes: Capture on every page_view or screen_view event and persist consistently across web and app. Prefer explicit CMS/template-driven values over URL parsing to avoid brittle logic. Maintain a controlled taxonomy with approved values such as home, category-listing, product-detail, search-results, cart, checkout, article, landing-page, account, support, and other. If both page_template and page_type exist, page_type should be the business-friendly reporting value while page_template can remain technical. Ensure SPAs update page_type on virtual page views. Avoid mixing content type and page intent unless taxonomy rules are documented.
business_use_case: Merchandising and UX teams use page type to compare conversion rate, bounce rate, and engagement across product-detail, category-listing, cart, and checkout pages to identify where users drop off and which templates need optimization.
source_data: Digital Analytics; Content Management System; Tag Management System

name: language
description: The language preference or locale-associated language used by the user, browser, app, or content experience. This dimension categorises traffic and behaviour by linguistic setting, enabling analysis of engagement, conversion, and experience quality across language audiences. Depending on implementation, it may represent browser language, device language, account language preference, or page/content language.
formula: If explicit user/account language exists, use that; else use application or page language; else use browser/device language. Example derivation: COALESCE(user_profile.language_preference, event_params.page_language, device.browser_language). For normalization, map locale values such as 'en-US' and 'en-GB' to base language 'en' when reporting at language level, or retain full locale when locale-level analysis is required.
tags: language; locale; internationalization; i18n; localization; audience; segmentation; content; browser-language; user-preference
industry: Retail; E-commerce; SaaS; Healthcare; Education; Finance; Media; Technology; Manufacturing; Other
aggregation_window: Event, Session, User, Time based - Hourly/Daily/Monthly/Yearly
sql_query:
SELECT
  event_date,
  user_pseudo_id,
  event_name,
  COALESCE(
    (SELECT value.string_value FROM UNNEST(user_properties) WHERE key = 'language'),
    (SELECT value.string_value FROM UNNEST(event_params) WHERE key = 'language'),
    device.language
  ) AS language
FROM `project.dataset.events_*`
WHERE _TABLE_SUFFIX BETWEEN '20240101' AND '20240131';
calculation_notes: Standardize values before reporting. Decide whether the canonical dimension stores full locale (e.g. en-US) or base language (e.g. en). If both are needed, create separate dimensions for language and locale. Capture as early as possible on first page_view/session_start and persist as a user property when it reflects stable preference. Browser language may differ from selected site language, so define source precedence clearly. Normalize casing and separators, e.g. convert en_US to en-US. Avoid mixing content language and user preference in the same field unless explicitly documented.
business_use_case: A global e-commerce team compares conversion rate, average order value, and checkout abandonment for users with language set to 'fr' versus 'en' to identify whether French-speaking visitors need improved translated product pages and checkout messaging.
source_data: Digital Analytics, Business Intelligence, CRM, CMS, Application Settings

name: Device Type
description: Categorises traffic and user interactions by the primary class of device used to access the digital property, typically grouping activity into desktop, mobile, tablet, smart TV, console, or other connected device categories. This dimension is used to compare behaviour, conversion, engagement, and performance across device classes.
formula: Derived from user agent parsing, SDK device classification, or analytics platform device category logic. Example logic: CASE WHEN lower(device_category) IN ('desktop','mobile','tablet','smart tv','tv','console') THEN normalized_device_category ELSE 'other' END.
tags: device; device category; platform; technology; user agent; cross-device; traffic analysis
industry: Retail; E-commerce; SaaS; Healthcare; Education; Finance; Media; Technology; Manufacturing; Other
aggregation_window: Event, Session, User, Time based - Hourly/Daily/Monthly/Yearly
xdm_mapping: {"path":"xdm.device.type","source":"device.type","notes":"Map normalized device class values such as desktop, mobile, tablet, tv, console, other."}
sql_query:
SELECT
  event_date,
  user_pseudo_id,
  ga_session_id,
  COALESCE(device.category,
    CASE
      WHEN LOWER(user_agent) LIKE '%mobile%' THEN 'mobile'
      WHEN LOWER(user_agent) LIKE '%tablet%' OR LOWER(user_agent) LIKE '%ipad%' THEN 'tablet'
      WHEN LOWER(user_agent) LIKE '%smart-tv%' OR LOWER(user_agent) LIKE '%hbbtv%' THEN 'tv'
      ELSE 'desktop'
    END
  ) AS device_type,
  COUNT(*) AS events
FROM raw_ga4_events
GROUP BY 1,2,3,4;
calculation_notes: Capture on every event or page view using platform-native device detection where available. Prefer analytics platform device classification over custom parsing to maintain consistency in reporting. If using custom logic, normalize values to a controlled taxonomy and avoid mixing operating system with device type. For apps, map handset and tablet form factors separately. For responsive web, device type should reflect actual device category, not viewport breakpoint. Persist at session scope for stable attribution within a visit. Validate edge cases such as foldables, bots, embedded browsers, and smart TVs.
business_use_case: Marketing and product teams use Device Type to compare conversion rate, bounce rate, average order value, and page speed between mobile and desktop sessions, helping prioritise UX fixes and media budget allocation.
dependencies: Page View event; Session ID; User Agent or SDK device metadata; Traffic metrics; Conversion metrics
report_attributes: Dimensions: Device Type, Device Category; Metrics: Users, Sessions, Events, Engagement Rate, Conversion Rate, Revenue; KPIs: Mobile Conversion Rate, Desktop Revenue Share
dashboard_usage: Executive Performance; Traffic Analysis; Conversion Optimisation; UX Performance; Ecommerce Trading
related_dimensions: Operating System; Browser; Screen Resolution; Platform; App Version; Device Brand
derived_dimensions: Mobile vs Desktop Grouping; Device Family; Cross-Device User Type; Device Performance Tier

name: New/Repeat
description: Classifies a user or session based on whether the visitor is interacting with the digital property for the first time ('New') or has interacted previously ('Repeat'). This dimension is commonly used to distinguish first-time acquisition performance from returning audience behavior, engagement, and conversion patterns.
formula: CASE WHEN user_first_touch_timestamp IS NULL THEN 'Unknown' WHEN event_timestamp = user_first_touch_timestamp OR session_number = 1 OR is_first_visit = TRUE THEN 'New' ELSE 'Repeat' END
tags: new visitor; repeat visitor; returning user; audience type; lifecycle; retention; acquisition; user status
industry: Retail; E-commerce; SaaS; Healthcare; Education; Finance; Media; Technology; Manufacturing; Other
aggregation_window: Event, Session, User, Time based - Hourly/Daily/Monthly/Yearly
ga4_event: new_vs_returning (custom user property if needed); derived from first_visit, first_open, ga_session_number, user_pseudo_id
xdm_mapping: {"xdm:identityMap":"Used with ECID/GA-style device identity to resolve visitor history","xdm:person":{"xdm:firstLoginDate":"2026-04-09T10:15:00Z"},"xdm:application":{"xdm:sessionLength":1},"_experience":{"analytics":{"customDimensions":{"eVars":{"eVar12":"New"}}}}}
sql_query:
WITH user_sessions AS (
  SELECT
    user_id,
    session_id,
    MIN(event_timestamp) AS session_start_ts,
    MIN(event_timestamp) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id) AS first_touch_ts,
    DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY MIN(event_timestamp)) AS session_number
  FROM raw_digital_events
  GROUP BY user_id, session_id
)
SELECT
  user_id,
  session_id,
  session_start_ts,
  CASE
    WHEN user_id IS NULL THEN 'Unknown'
    WHEN session_number = 1 OR session_start_ts = first_touch_ts THEN 'New'
    ELSE 'Repeat'
  END AS new_repeat
FROM user_sessions;
calculation_notes: Prefer user-level classification using a durable identity such as user_id, CRM ID, or stitched identity graph. If only device/browser identifiers are available, this dimension will reflect device-level new/repeat status rather than person-level status. In GA4, standard reporting often derives new/returning from first_visit/first_open and session behavior; for warehouse models, calculate from the earliest known event per user. Persist the value at session start to avoid mid-session changes. Define a fallback value such as 'Unknown' when consent restrictions, cookie loss, ITP, app reinstall, or identity resets prevent reliable history lookup. For Adobe, use an eVar with visit or visitor expiration depending on reporting need; visitor expiration is preferred for stable user classification.
business_use_case: Marketing and product teams use New/Repeat to compare conversion rate, revenue per user, and engagement between first-time visitors and returning audiences. Example: an e-commerce team filters checkout conversion by New vs Repeat to determine whether paid social is driving net-new customers or mostly re-engaging existing shoppers.
dependencies: user_id or user_pseudo_id; session_id; event_timestamp; first_visit or first_open event; session_number; identity stitching rules; consent status
report_attributes: Dimensions: New/Repeat, User Type, Session Number; Metrics: Users, Sessions, Engaged Sessions, Conversion Rate, Revenue, AOV; KPIs: New User Conversion Rate, Repeat User Revenue Share, Retention Lift
dashboard_usage: Executive KPI Overview;Traffic Analysis;Audience Insights;Retention Dashboard;Conversion Funnel;Lifecycle Performance
related_dimensions: User ID;Session Number;First Visit Date;Customer Type;Traffic Source;Device Category;Logged-in Status
derived_dimensions: New User Rate;Repeat User Rate;Repeat Purchase Segment;Returning Session Share;Customer Lifecycle Stage
aliases: visitor type; user type; new vs returning; new/returning; first-time vs repeat; returning visitor

name: Login status
description: Categorises whether the user is authenticated at the time of the event or session. Typical values include logged-in, logged-out, guest, remembered-user, and unknown. This dimension is used to compare behaviour, conversion, retention, and content consumption between authenticated and unauthenticated audiences.
formula: Derived from authentication state at event time. Example logic: CASE WHEN user_id IS NOT NULL OR auth_state IN ('authenticated','logged_in') THEN 'logged-in' WHEN auth_state IN ('guest','anonymous','logged_out') THEN 'logged-out' ELSE 'unknown' END
tags: authentication; user-state; identity; segmentation; login; member-status; visitor-status
industry: Retail; E-commerce; SaaS; Healthcare; Education; Finance; Media; Technology; Manufacturing; Other
aggregation_window: Event, Session, User, Time based - Hourly/Daily/Monthly/Yearly
sql_query:
SELECT
  event_date,
  event_timestamp,
  user_pseudo_id,
  COALESCE(
    (SELECT up.value.string_value FROM UNNEST(user_properties) up WHERE up.key = 'login_status'),
    CASE
      WHEN user_id IS NOT NULL THEN 'logged-in'
      ELSE 'logged-out'
    END
  ) AS login_status
FROM `project.dataset.events_*`;
calculation_notes: Capture this value on every event as a persistent user property and, where possible, also stamp it as an event parameter to preserve historical state changes. Do not infer logged-in solely from presence of CRM identifiers in downstream systems; use front-end or server-side authentication state at collection time. Define a controlled value set such as logged-in, logged-out, guest, remembered-user, unknown and enforce it across web and app. If a user logs in mid-session, event-level values should reflect the exact state before and after login, while session-level reporting should use either session-start state or a documented precedence rule. Avoid sending raw usernames, emails, account IDs, or tokens. In GA4, user properties are not retroactive, so event parameter duplication is recommended for accurate historical analysis. In Adobe, use an eVar with visit or visitor expiration depending on reporting need; if login can change within a visit, also capture in a prop for pathing. In AEP/XDM, map to a custom field group rather than overloading identity namespaces if your implementation requires strict schema governance.
business_use_case: A product team compares conversion rate, feature adoption, and support article usage between logged-in and logged-out users to identify whether authentication barriers are suppressing engagement. Marketing teams use it to exclude logged-in customers from acquisition campaign landing page analysis. Subscription businesses use it to evaluate whether remembered users are more likely to return and convert than fully anonymous visitors.

name: Login Type
description: Categorises the authentication method or account access path a user used when logging in or authenticating within a digital property. Typical values include password, single-sign-on, social login, magic link, biometric, passkey, OTP, guest, or unknown. This dimension is used to analyse authentication behaviour, friction, security adoption, and downstream conversion or retention differences by login method.
formula: Derived from the authentication method captured at login or account access events. Example logic: CASE WHEN auth_method IN ('password','email_password') THEN 'Password' WHEN auth_method IN ('google','apple','facebook','linkedin','social') THEN 'Social Login' WHEN auth_method IN ('sso','saml','oauth_enterprise','okta','azure_ad') THEN 'SSO' WHEN auth_method IN ('magic_link','email_link') THEN 'Magic Link' WHEN auth_method IN ('passkey','webauthn') THEN 'Passkey' WHEN auth_method IN ('otp','sms_otp','email_otp','mfa_code') THEN 'OTP' WHEN auth_method IN ('biometric','face_id','touch_id') THEN 'Biometric' WHEN auth_method IN ('guest','anonymous') THEN 'Guest' ELSE 'Unknown' END
tags: authentication; login; identity; access; account; segmentation; user-journey; security
industry: Retail; E-commerce; SaaS; Healthcare; Education; Finance; Media; Technology; Other
aggregation_window: Event, Session, User, Time based - Hourly/Daily/Monthly/Yearly
sql_query: SELECT event_date, user_pseudo_id, session_id, event_name, COALESCE((SELECT ep.value.string_value FROM UNNEST(event_params) ep WHERE ep.key = 'login_type'), CASE WHEN LOWER((SELECT ep.value.string_value FROM UNNEST(event_params) ep WHERE ep.key = 'auth_method')) IN ('password','email_password') THEN 'Password' WHEN LOWER((SELECT ep.value.string_value FROM UNNEST(event_params) ep WHERE ep.key = 'auth_method')) IN ('google','apple','facebook','linkedin','social') THEN 'Social Login' WHEN LOWER((SELECT ep.value.string_value FROM UNNEST(event_params) ep WHERE ep.key = 'auth_method')) IN ('sso','saml','oauth_enterprise','okta','azure_ad') THEN 'SSO' WHEN LOWER((SELECT ep.value.string_value FROM UNNEST(event_params) ep WHERE ep.key = 'auth_method')) IN ('magic_link','email_link') THEN 'Magic Link' WHEN LOWER((SELECT ep.value.string_value FROM UNNEST(event_params) ep WHERE ep.key = 'auth_method')) IN ('passkey','webauthn') THEN 'Passkey' WHEN LOWER((SELECT ep.value.string_value FROM UNNEST(event_params) ep WHERE ep.key = 'auth_method')) IN ('otp','sms_otp','email_otp','mfa_code') THEN 'OTP' WHEN LOWER((SELECT ep.value.string_value FROM UNNEST(event_params) ep WHERE ep.key = 'auth_method')) IN ('biometric','face_id','touch_id') THEN 'Biometric' WHEN LOWER((SELECT ep.value.string_value FROM UNNEST(event_params) ep WHERE ep.key = 'auth_method')) IN ('guest','anonymous') THEN 'Guest' ELSE 'Unknown' END) AS login_type FROM `project.dataset.events_*` WHERE event_name IN ('login','sign_in','authentication','session_start');
calculation_notes: Capture on successful login events at minimum; optionally also capture on login_attempt and login_failure for funnel diagnostics. Standardise values across web, app, and backend auth providers before ingestion. Do not infer from identity provider name unless a controlled mapping exists. Persist as a user-scoped latest-known attribute only if business needs require user-level reporting, but preserve event-level value because users may switch methods over time. Ensure guest access is distinguished from authenticated login. For SSO, map enterprise IdP variants consistently. Avoid storing provider-specific identifiers or email domains in this dimension. If MFA is a secondary factor rather than primary login type, decide whether to classify by primary method only or create a separate MFA dimension.
business_use_case: A product and security team compares conversion from login to purchase by Login Type and finds that Magic Link users complete checkout at a higher rate on mobile than Password users. They prioritise expanding passwordless authentication and use the dimension to monitor adoption and retention impact over time.
